item.title: A Sprawling Middle East War Explodes
item.description: A joint U.S.-Israeli attack on Iran, followed by Iranian retaliation against U.S. military assets, Israel, and other U.S. allies, has engulfed much of the Middle East in conflict. Crisis Group experts offer a 360-degree view of the new war’s initial reverberations in the region.

item.title: Arab States Scramble to Adapt to New Regional War
item.description: Since the United States and Israel began intensive airstrikes against Iran on February 28, Iran has retaliated with missile and drone strikes against multiple Arab countries, hitting U.S. military targets and civilian infrastructure. This marks a hard stop to a recent warming in relations between Iran and some Gulf Arab states, which are now trying to understand the war and adapt.

item.title: Operation Epic Folly? Trump’s and Netanyahu’s War on Iran
item.description: Crisis Group experts discuss the U.S.-Israel war with Iran, its fallout in the Gulf and wider region, and what might come next. The analysis focuses on the implications and potential future developments of the conflict.

item.title: A Worst-Case Scenario for the War with Iran
item.description: The war with Iran raises questions about the regime's survival, potential alternatives, the risk of chaos, and implications for the Arab Gulf states, Israel, Iraq, and Lebanon. While the White House anticipates a four-to-six-week duration, the article explores a worst-case scenario where events spiral beyond U.S. and Israeli control.

item.title: Lessons from Ukraine for Defending Gulf Airspace from Shaheds
item.description: In early March 2026, U.S. Central Command confirmed the first U.S. fatalities of the Iran conflict following a drone strike at Port Shuaiba in Kuwait. The article analyzes the challenges of defending against low-cost drones like Shaheds, highlighting the imbalance between target cost and interception cost, and drawing lessons from the Ukrainian experience.

item.title: Gaza and the Logic of High-Intensity Urban Warfare
item.description: Analyzing the Gaza War between Israel and Hamas requires an analytical framework suited to its character of high-intensity urban warfare. Much commentary measures the conflict against a perceived 'gold standard' of humane warfare, but the article argues this model, often drawn from interpretations of Western experiences in Iraq and Afghanistan, does not fully fit the reality of Gaza.

item.title: EU Mulls Capping Gas Price as Energy Costs Spike on Iran War
item.description: The European Union is considering emergency relief measures, including capping the price of natural gas, in response to the energy price spike amid the Middle East war. European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen stated that the surge in oil and gas prices has already cost European taxpayers an additional 3 billion euros in fossil fuel imports.

item.title: Iran Warns Oil Could Hit $200 per Barrel as Hormuz Threat Escalates
item.description: Iran warned that crude oil could surge to $200 per barrel if the war involving the U.S. and Israel continues to destabilize the Middle East’s energy corridors. An Iranian military spokesperson stated that regional security has been destabilized by the ongoing bombing campaign against Iran, urging the world to prepare for high oil prices.

item.title: IEA Launches Record 400-Million-Barrel Emergency Oil Release
item.description: The International Energy Agency (IEA) and its member nations have agreed to release a record 400 million barrels of oil from strategic reserves to stabilize global energy markets. This coordinated action, the largest emergency oil release ever organized by the IEA, comes as the war involving Iran continues to disrupt crude flows through the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Tankers Avoid Emirati Port as Gulf War Risk Disrupts Oil Exports
item.description: Shipping companies have grown reluctant to call at the port of Fujairah in the UAE, prompting cancellations of oil cargoes that Adnoc is then reselling at higher prices. Although Fujairah is outside the Strait of Hormuz, its proximity to the conflict zone has led to disruptions, with the port having been targeted several times since the war started.

item.title: Aramco Asks Asian Buyers for Dual Red Sea-Hormuz Oil Supply Plans
item.description: Saudi Arabia’s oil giant Aramco is requesting Asian buyers to nominate crude loading plans for April for both its key export port in the Gulf and the export alternative on the Red Sea. This move reflects the crisis at the Strait of Hormuz, which has forced Saudi Arabia to divert part of its crude oil exports from the Ras Tanura terminal to the Yanbu export port on the Red Sea.

item.title: Iranian Drone Strike Hits Oman’s Largest Oil Storage Facility
item.description: Iranian drones struck oil storage facilities at the Port of Salalah in Oman, marking the latest attack on Gulf energy infrastructure as the regional war expands. Fuel storage tanks at the port were hit, although no merchant vessels in the area were damaged.

item.title: Stock market today: Dow, S&P 500, Nasdaq slip amid fresh inflation data, continued Iran fallout
item.description: Major U.S. stock indices, including the Dow, S&P 500, and Nasdaq, slipped today. This movement is attributed to both fresh inflation data and ongoing concerns and economic fallout from the continuing conflict with Iran, which is impacting global market stability.

item.title: Stock Market Today: Dow Sinks As Oil Spikes Amid U.S.-Iran War; Defense Stock Falls (Live Coverage)
item.description: The Dow Jones experienced a significant decline today, while oil prices surged sharply due to the escalating U.S.-Iran war. Despite the conflict backdrop, defense stocks showed a fall, indicating a complex market reaction to geopolitical events and inflation data.

item.title: How Will This Energy Shock Play Out?
item.description: Although the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran is unprecedented, previous energy and financial crises offer useful lessons for investors and policymakers. The article suggests that oil and natural gas producers cannot assume prices will stay high indefinitely, analyzing historical dynamics and potential future trajectories of the current crisis.
